narrative_blocks.header: Tallmadge's junior officer became Ohio's militia general
narrative_blocks.body: Elijah Wadsworth served through the Revolution under Benjamin Tallmadge, ending the war a captain. Decades later, on the Western Reserve, he was commissioned major-general of the Ohio militia. The frontier soldier had become the frontier's general.

narrative_blocks.header: The house he built became Harriet Beecher Stowe's
narrative_blocks.body: The frame house Wadsworth raised at Canfield in 1803 was the first in the township. Generations later it passed into the hands of the Beecher family. The pioneer's homestead outlived the pioneer by a long way.

narrative_blocks.header: An original signer of the Connecticut Land Company
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1795 Wadsworth was among the signers of the contract that opened the Western Reserve to settlement. He spent the summer of 1799 scouting Canfield and the surrounding towns. By 1802 he had moved his whole family west by horse and ox team.

narrative_blocks.header: He held the frontier after Hull's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: When war was declared in 1812 and General Hull surrendered Detroit, Wadsworth was called into active service as a major-general. He threw his full energy into defending the exposed Ohio frontier. Contemporaries credited him with a real service to the country.

narrative_blocks.header: A whole Ohio city now bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: Wadsworth, Ohio, in Medina County, is named for the general. He had once owned a significant portion of the land on which the city grew. His name outlasted his fortune, which the Revolution had consumed entirely.

cross_connections.display_label: Served under Tallmadge in Sheldon's Horse — both ended up in the Western Reserve
Harriet Beecher Stowe was born in 1811 in the Litchfield house Wadsworth built, after it passed to her father Rev. Lyman Beecher.
largest buyer of that township; Canfield, Ohio named for him

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Connecticut Land Company; agent for Canfield Township 1799-1802.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built it in 1774; sold to Lyman Beecher in 1810 — Harriet Beecher Stowe was born there in 1811.
The 1799 Litchfield house he built, now the Oliver Wolcott Library.

military_service.notes: Served directly under Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge. Co-founded Sheldon's Horse. Served Long Island, Monmouth, Yorktown. Was at West Point when Major André was taken prisoner, 1780.

Commanded Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) as aide. Organized 3,000-man militia; built forts and roads across NE Ohio. Commanded at Battle of the Peninsula, Sep 10, 1812. Resigned Feb 1814 due to age/health. Personal debt of $26,551 supplying troops — Congress discharged 1825.

career.notes: Recalled to active service in the War of 1812

career.notes: Recalled to active service in the War of 1812

narrative_blocks.header: He was an early mayor of Akron
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick Wadsworth helped develop frontier Ohio through wool, canal, and iron ventures before serving as mayor of Akron in 1852. He had moved west from Litchfield, Connecticut, as a boy of thirteen.

His father, General Elijah Wadsworth, appointed him a lieutenant in the Ohio militia at the outbreak of the War of 1812. The Wadsworth house he built in Edinburg Township still stands on the National Register.

narrative_blocks.header: He conquered a long battle with drink
narrative_blocks.body: For some twenty-five years Frederick Wadsworth struggled with intemperance, then gave it up entirely and never drank again. He became a fervent advocate of abstinence, saying once that five hundred dollars could not tempt him to a single drink.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped finance the rise of Akron
narrative_blocks.body: A canal commissioner and investor, Wadsworth joined Simon Perkins in the Portage Canal and Manufacturing Company that powered the new boomtown. He even lent money to the young John Brown for an Ohio farm before Brown's speculation collapsed in the Panic of 1837.

sources.full: Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication

INST0045.name: Connecticut Land Company
INST0045.notes: Formed 1795 by 36 Connecticut investors who purchased the entire Connecticut Western Reserve from the State of Connecticut for over $1,000,000 — land the state had 'reserved' for citizens whose property was damaged by the British in the Revolution. In April 1807, Richfield Township was acquired for $26,087 by seven men: Col. Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757), Solomon Lathrop, Jeremiah Wilcox, Uriel Holmes Jr., Pierpont Edwards (H00386), Daniel Green, and Jonathan Dwight Sr. — two of the seven being Hooker descendants. Pierpont Edwards (H00386) invested $60,000 — approximately 5% of total company holdings. His son John Stark Edwards (HD2044) served as his first agent in the Reserve, but died Feb 22, 1813 in Warren, Trumbull County OH age 35 — the year Whittlesey rode through the black swamp and nearly died at Fort Meigs. Pierpont Township, Ashtabula County, Ohio is named for Edwards. In 1811, Heman Oviatt (husband of HD2934 Eunice Newton Oviatt) bought the northwest quarter of Richfield Township from Col. Tallmadge for $1.25 an acre. John Newton (HD2925) arrived in Richfield in the early 1820s and purchased most of the east center land, building the home later owned by Homer Carter. Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) arrived in Canfield June 27, 1806 — the same summer the company was active — and became public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve. The company was ultimately a financial failure. (Richfield Township History; Ohio History Journal; Whittlesey Genealogy #310)

INST035.name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
INST035.notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.
